title: AI Agent Risk, Oversight, and Failure

modules.title: The Failure Landscape: How Autonomous Agents Go Wrong
modules.sub: Mapping the real ways AI agents break and cause harm
modules.description: Learners survey the spectrum of AI agent failures — from silent errors to cascading system damage — and develop a shared vocabulary for categorizing them. By the end of this module, learners can identify which failure types are most relevant to their own deployment context.

modules.title: Prompt Injection, Goal Misgeneralization, and Emergent Behavior
modules.sub: Understanding adversarial and unexpected agent behaviors in depth
modules.description: Learners examine three high-stakes failure mechanisms that are unique to autonomous agents and often misunderstood by non-technical stakeholders. They analyze realistic scenarios to recognize the conditions that make each failure mode likely and how early signals might be detected.

modules.title: Designing Human-in-the-Loop Controls for Agentic Systems
modules.sub: Building meaningful human checkpoints without strangling autonomy
modules.description: Learners evaluate tradeoffs between agent autonomy and human oversight, then design checkpoint structures appropriate to their organization's risk tolerance. They produce a draft oversight map that specifies when, how, and by whom human review is triggered in an agent workflow.

modules.title: Monitoring, Logging, and Detecting Drift in Production Agents
modules.sub: Keeping watch on agents after they leave the building
modules.description: Learners identify the key telemetry signals that indicate an agent is behaving outside acceptable parameters and explore logging strategies that make post-incident analysis tractable. They design a basic monitoring spec for a production agent scenario, defining what to capture and what thresholds should trigger alerts.

modules.title: Liability, Accountability, and Organizational Governance
modules.sub: Deciding who owns agent decisions when things go wrong
modules.description: Learners work through realistic accountability gaps — situations where an agent causes harm and no clear owner exists — and apply governance frameworks to close them. They draft a responsibility assignment model that maps agent actions to specific human roles within an organization.

modules.title: Risk Frameworks: Evaluating Agents Before Deployment
modules.sub: Applying structured methods to rate and gate agent deployments
modules.description: Learners are introduced to established and emerging risk evaluation frameworks applicable to AI agents and practice scoring an agent system against key criteria. They leave with a repeatable pre-deployment evaluation process they can adapt for their own organization's risk standards.

modules.title: Case Studies: High-Profile Agent Failures and What They Cost
modules.sub: Learning from real incidents before you become one
modules.description: Learners analyze documented real-world agent failures, tracing each incident from root cause through organizational and financial consequence. They extract transferable lessons and identify which failure vectors and governance gaps appear repeatedly across different industries and system types.

modules.title: Apply It: Conduct a Risk Audit of an Agent in Your Organization
modules.sub: Putting every framework to work on a real agent system
modules.description: Learners select an AI agent system from their own organization — or a provided realistic scenario — and conduct a full structured risk audit using the tools built throughout the course. They deliver a completed audit report that identifies failure vectors, assigns accountability, and specifies actionable oversight controls ready for stakeholder review.

synopsis: Deploying AI agents without a structured risk and oversight model is how organizations create expensive, embarrassing, or dangerous failures â often without seeing them coming. This course equips managers, architects, and domain experts with the frameworks to evaluate agent risk, design meaningful oversight checkpoints, and build accountable AI systems.
rationale: As agentic AI deployment accelerates, the professionals responsible for oversight and governance urgently need structured mental models for agent failure modes that go well beyond standard AI ethics talking points.
learning_outcome: Conduct a structured risk audit of an AI agent system â identifying failure vectors, assigning accountability, and specifying actionable oversight controls before or after deployment.
